title: Create a navigation menu with relative URLs

source.module: Core Django Fundamentals – Templates & Static Content

topicTags: Core Django Fundamentals – Templates & Static Content

content.problemStatement:
In this worked-out example, we will help you understand the importance of relative URLs.

### Try this out
Run the code - when you click on the links - 'Home', 'About' and 'Contact' - you will see that it loads the respective routes `/home/`, `/about/` and `/contact/`

Now - make the following change in `urls.py`. Replace the following url pattern with the new url pattern
```python
# Existing urlpattern
urlpatterns = [
    path('', views.home, name='home'),
    path('home/', views.home, name='home'),
    path('about/', views.about, name='about'),
    path('contact/', views.contact, name='contact'),
]

# New urlpattern
urlpatterns = [
    path('', views.home, name='home'),
    path('home-new/', views.home, name='home'),
    path('about-us/', views.about, name='about'),
    path('contact-us/', views.contact, name='contact'),
]
```
Run the code and click on the 3 links again.

### What do you notice?
- When we click on 'Home' - we get an error. We get this error because its path was hardcoded in `base.html`
- When we click 'About' or 'Contact' - we dont get an error. This is because we used Relative URLs in `base.html` for these.
